name: James Buffington
abstract: James Lawrence Buffington (May 15, 1922, Jersey Shore, Pennsylvania – July 20, 1981, Englewood, New Jersey) was an American jazz, studio, and classical  hornist. Buffington was a busy studio and jazz player on the French horn.

works.t: Quintet for Piano and Winds in E-flat Major, K. 452

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Dizzy Gillespie: Genius at Midnight

works.t: Serenade in B-flat Major, K. 361, "Gran Partita"

concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Jelly Roll Morton

works.t: Variations for Solo Tuba and Nine Horns

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: I had my two best jazz horn colleagues, Jimmy Buffington and John Barrows, play horn whenever I used horns.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Gap Mangione
about_quotes.evidence: And then Frank Wess, Joe Farrell, Jerome Richardson, Mike Mainieri, Tony Levin, Steve Gadd — oh and Jimmy Buffington and Earl Chapin, two of the best horn players that ever existed.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gap Mangione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Rochester, New York, January 11, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: later, in New York, I, along with Johnny Barrows, Jim Buffington, and of course Julius Watkins, we were sort of the pioneers of playing jazz at all
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Varner
about_quotes.evidence: like Gunther Schuller or like Jimmy Buffington. But people who are real improvisers, there weren't that many at the time when I was say 17 or 18.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Varner interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, August 13, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Shiner
about_quotes.evidence: I saw Ed in the trombone section and Jim Buffington, a French horn player was there.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Shiner interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 11, 1998
